title: The People's Welfare Workers Are Tamils Too!
translatorNote: This translation is intended to carry Kalaignar's voice into clear, contemporary English rather than recast the letter as literary or academic prose. It preserves the source's argument, political directness, rhetorical questions, repetition, irony, factual detail, and paragraph order. Names, dates, figures, quotations, and intentional English expressions are retained. Where Tamil idiom cannot be reproduced literally without sounding unnatural, the English follows its sense and rhetorical force without adding claims absent from the source. The original Tamil is reproduced in full below the translation and remains the authoritative text. `Udanpirappē` is retained in Tamil transliteration rather than flattened into “brother,” “sister,” or “comrade.” Literally evoking “one born alongside me,” Kalaignar uses it as a distinctive address of shared identity, equality, affection, and solidarity within the movement. The OCR-derived text repeated a corrupted fragment of the title before the salutation and severely damaged two lines in the 29 November 2011 Supreme Court passage. The source pages show that the letter begins directly with `உடன்பிறப்பே`, and the damaged passage has been restored from the visible print in the derived Tamil reading copy. The canonical source files remain unchanged.
paragraphs: When I became Chief Minister under the Dravida Munnetra Kazhagam government in 1989, 13,000 People's Welfare Workers were appointed on 2 July 1990. We did so with the good intention of creating employment and a livelihood for unemployed young people.

For the sole reason that they had been appointed under a DMK government, the AIADMK government that came to office in 1991 turned its anger upon them. It dismissed all 13,000 in May, took away their livelihood and sent them home. When the DMK returned to office under my leadership in 1996, those 13,000 workers were given employment again. But when Jayalalithaa returned to power in 2001, they were once more told there was no work and sent home on 31 May 2001.

When the DMK government was formed under my leadership in 2006, the People's Welfare Workers were given their jobs back on 31 May 2006, restoring and protecting their lives and livelihoods. Thus, three times in succession, whenever a DMK government led by me came to office, the workers were protected through employment; whenever Jayalalithaa became Chief Minister, they were abandoned and sent home.
Their repeated denial of work and dismissal led to the shocking deaths by suicide of nineteen people.

To give only a few examples: Veerappan, a People's Welfare Worker from Kameswaram village in Vairavankattalai near Keezhaiyur, Nagapattinam district, consumed poison and died by suicide on 11 June 2001 after losing his job. Prabha, wife of C. Kandasamy, a People's Welfare Worker in Oddanchatram union, Dindigul district, consumed poison and died that same day after her husband lost his work. On 12 June 2001, Thilagar, who had served as a People's Welfare Worker in Natham union, also took his life, unable to endure the pain. The DMK gave each of their families Rs 25,000 in relief at the time.

After losing his job, Ramamoorthy of Aruppukkottai stopped eating in grief and died. Ilangovan of Tharangambadi in Nagapattinam district consumed poison and died. Kabilan of Mandapam in Ramanathapuram district, anguished by the loss of his job, starved himself and died. The list goes on. The rulers neither showed compassion nor listened to any protest organised by the dismissed People's Welfare Workers.

Immediately after the AIADMK assumed office in May 2011, the Tamil Nadu government dismissed more than 13,000 People's Welfare Workers through Government Order No. 86 dated 8 November 2011. On 15 November 2011, the DMK Youth Wing held a demonstration in Chennai under the leadership of Thalapathi Thambi M. K. Stalin, demanding that the injustice inflicted upon them by the AIADMK government be undone and their jobs restored.

Office-bearers of their association challenged the dismissal order in the High Court. Justice Suguna granted an interim stay. When the case was heard on 21 November 2011, the court was told that its order had not been implemented and the workers had not been reinstated. Justice Suguna ordered that they be permitted to rejoin immediately and that a report confirming implementation of the order be filed on 23 November 2011.

An election to the People's Welfare Workers' Association was held on 3 April 2012, and Mathivanan was elected president and Rajendran secretary. Although the former office-bearer Palani had been removed in that election, he failed to hand the case files to the newly elected administrators.

Any important decision on behalf of the association had to be taken only after convening its executive committee. Yet after he had ceased to be the association's secretary, Palani consulted no one and entered into an agreement by himself. He agreed that payment of five months' wages would be sufficient and consented to the workers' dismissal, then informed the court of that agreement. The court accepted it as genuine and approved the order dismissing the People's Welfare Workers. Only afterwards did the newly elected association officers discover the fraud Palani had committed against them.
Counsel Wilson, appearing for the workers, then argued that Palani held no office, Mathivanan was the lawful office-bearer, and therefore the agreement made with Palani must be cancelled.
The Tamil Nadu government appealed against Justice Suguna's order. The First Bench comprising Chief Justice Iqbal and Justice Sivagnanam heard the appeal and said:

> It is apparent that the People's Welfare Workers have been appointed and removed according to the government's wishes. Even if, for argument's sake, they are treated as temporary employees, they have worked for the past twenty years. If they were to be dismissed with a single signature, the government, in accordance with justice and law, should first have given them notice and heard their response. It did not do so. > > Justice Suguna correctly took this into account when granting the stay, and it cannot be vacated. The condition of the People's Welfare Workers resembles a football being kicked about. > > Once a person has been given government work with pay fixed in accordance with a Supreme Court order, removing that person without valid reason is illegal. We strongly condemn this. Issuing contradictory orders whenever the government changes is deeply condemnable, painful and regrettable. We therefore reject the government's request. > > If a new government changes a scheme introduced by the previous government, it must place those employees in other work. Dismissing them instead is wrong. How can this be just? We can see that it is politically motivated, though we do not wish to intervene in that aspect.

The Tamil Nadu government nevertheless appealed this judgment in the Supreme Court. On 29 November 2011, Justices D. K. Jain and Dave heard the petition and asked: “What is happening in Tamil Nadu? Why are the previous government's schemes cancelled whenever the government changes? Such things must not happen in Tamil Nadu again.” They expressed their condemnation. The Supreme Court Division Bench then rejected the request by the Tamil Nadu government's counsel to hear the matter as an urgent case.

This case returned to the Supreme Court on 11 November 2013. A Bench comprising Justices Anil R. Dave and Dipak Misra set aside the High Court order permitting the People's Welfare Workers to be dismissed. It ruled:

> The case must be reheard, considering every issue on its merits, and concluded within six months. The Tamil Nadu government must not seek adjournments. By entering into an agreement with someone who held no responsibility, the government has ruined the lives of 13,000 People's Welfare Workers. This is condemnable. The government must remember that they devoted twenty years of their lives to its service.

Udanpirappē, does a government in a democracy need a condemnation more severe than this? Who are those 13,000 People's Welfare Workers? Are they my relatives? People known to me? Members of my party? Were they not born in Tamil Nadu? Are they not all Tamils?

How many people in their families survived on the wages of those 13,000 workers? What benefit can one individual woman gain by earning the anguish of all those stomachs? Is it right for a woman Chief Minister to harbour such hostility merely because they received employment under a DMK government? How many judges have delivered judgments in this case, and how strongly? Should reflection upon all this not have brought a change of heart?

Just as these workers were appointed under a DMK government, many people are appointed under AIADMK governments. Were all of them removed when a DMK government came to office? Have they not all continued in their jobs?
Honourable judges have asked, “What is happening in Tamil Nadu?” and, “Is it right to kick the People's Welfare Workers about like a ball?” Does this government not consider that a humiliation?

Let what has happened remain in the past. At least now, the AIADMK government must come to its senses. It must abandon vindictive action against anyone. Although the Supreme Court has ordered the High Court to hear and conclude the workers' case within six months, the Tamil Nadu government should not wait for that hearing. It should come forward on its own, withdraw the case and immediately restore employment to all 13,000 People's Welfare Workers.

“Anger must cool” is an old saying. We must not forget that preserving one's anger without letting it cool will lead into the deepest abyss. The AIADMK government must let go of its anger against the People's Welfare Workers merely because they were appointed under a DMK government. It must come forward to protect them and their families.
With affection,
M. K.
14 November 2013
